target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: docs(browser): resolve networkidle contradiction across browser docs
target.body:
## Summary

- `docs/tools/browser-control.md` advertises `openclaw browser wait --load networkidle` without any profile qualifier, while `docs/tools/browser.md` and `docs/cli/browser.md` say it is not supported — with no qualifier on the "not supported" side to indicate the restriction is profile-scoped. Reported in #80587.
- Qualifies all three docs pages to state that `networkidle` works on managed `openclaw` and raw/remote CDP profiles, but not on `existing-session`/`user` profiles. The runtime already enforces this boundary (`existing-session-limits.ts:29` rejects `networkidle` for existing-session waits; `pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1240` supports it on Playwright-backed profiles).

## Linked context

Closes #80587

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Docs contradiction on `browser wait --load networkidle` support.
- Real environment tested: Local checkout on `upstream/main` (8142c12db2).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```text
rg -n 'networkidle' docs/tools/browser-control.md docs/tools/browser.md docs/cli/browser.md
```
- Evidence after fix:
```text
docs/cli/browser.md:318:- `wait --load networkidle` is not supported on existing-session profiles (works on managed and raw/remote CDP)
docs/tools/browser.md:746:- **Wait / upload / dialog** - `wait --url` supports exact, substring, and glob patterns; `wait --load networkidle` is not supported on existing-session profiles (it works on managed and raw/remote CDP profiles). [...]
docs/tools/browser-control.md:324:  - `openclaw browser wait --load networkidle`
docs/tools/browser-control.md:325:  - Supported on managed `openclaw` and raw/remote CDP profiles. The `user` and `existing-session` profiles reject `networkidle`; use `--url`, `--text`, a selector, or `--fn` waits there.
```
  All three pages now carry a profile-scoped qualifier; no unqualified "is not supported" or unqualified positive example remains.
- Observed result after fix: Contradiction resolved — each page states the exact profile boundary.
- What was not tested: No live browser session was needed; this is a docs-only change verified by reading the patched Markdown.
- Proof limitations: Docs accuracy depends on the runtime boundary staying unchanged; I confirmed the runtime guard (`existing-session-limits.ts:29`, `agent.act.ts:349`) and Playwright wait path (`pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1240`) still enforce the same split on current `upstream/main`.

## Tests and validation

- `git diff --check` — clean (no whitespace errors)
- Diff is 3 files, +3/-2 lines — no runtime, config, or code changes

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No` (docs-only)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

Highest-risk area: Docs accuracy if the runtime boundary changes in a future release

How is that risk mitigated? The profile-scoped wording matches the current runtime contract; a future runtime change would naturally require a docs update anyway.

Current review state: maintainer review and CI.

AI-assisted (Claude Code). Proof above was verified by manual terminal grep on patched files.

target.title: fix: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typin...
target.body:
## Summary

Found one concrete regression: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typing indicator has finished starting, so the stop path can no-op and leave the indicator behind.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: Pending typing start can outlive cleanup and leave persistent indicators stuck** (regression)
  - File: `src/channels/typing.ts:79`
  - Evidence: `onReplyStart` now starts `fireStart()` in the background and returns after one microtask at `src/channels/typing.ts:79-87`, while `fireStop` immediately marks the callbacks closed and calls `stop` at `src/channels/typing.ts:90-98`. For Feishu, `start` assigns `typingState` only after `await addTypingIndicator(...)` at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:176`; `stop` returns without doing anything if `typingState` is still unset at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:183-185`. A fast cleanup while `addTypingIndicator` is still in flight therefore calls stop too early, then the delayed start completes and records a persistent reaction with no later removal.
  - Impact: Feishu typing reactions are explicitly persistent until removed. A quick reply, `NO_REPLY`, empty-message cleanup, cancellation, or send-policy path can leave a visible typing reaction/indicator on the parent message after the run is already finished.
  - Suggested fix: keep typing start off the reply critical path, but serialize cleanup against the pending start. For example, track the current start promise and, if stop/cleanup happens while it is pending, run `stop` again after the promise settles when a start actually completed. Alternatively make persistent-indicator channels expose an abortable/pending-aware start helper.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/channels/typing.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`
- `src/channels/typing.test.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d
- Latest main at intake: 8093ae60292f4d9009b3d48f234b39b5cbc538e5
- Original commit author: Ayaan Zaidi
- GitHub author: @obviyus
- Highest severity: medium
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `40b0b1bfe051072f47d0160eb078afa3b17cef48..45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d`
- Changed files: `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.test.ts`
- Code read: changed files in full; `src/channels/typing-lifecycle.ts`; `src/channels/typing-start-guard.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`; relevant channel typing call sites, including Feishu, Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Signal, Mattermost, MSTeams, Zalo, and heartbeat typing.
- Dependencies/web: no dependency files changed; no web lookup needed.

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm install` was needed because `vitest/package.json` was missing.
- `pnpm test src/channels/typing.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/typing-persistence.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` passed.
- Focused `pnpm exec tsx -e ...` race probe reproduced the stale persistent-state ordering.

Known review limits:
- I did not run a live Feishu API test; the finding is based on the current callback ordering and Feishu’s local persistent-reaction state contract.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

ClawSweeper 🐠 replacement reef notes:
- Cluster: clawsweeper-commit-openclaw-openclaw-45b86450795d
- Source PRs: none
- Credit: Detected by ClawSweeper commit review for 45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d.; Original commit author: Ayaan Zaidi.
- Validation: pnpm check:changed

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` - extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Compatibility and exported surface (maintainer sign-off requested)

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.
- **Exported draft-loop surface.** This adds an optional `takePending?(): string` member to the exported `DraftStreamLoop` type (`src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound`). It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Diff size

```
 docs/channels/mattermost.md                        |   6 +-
 docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md                   |  16 +-
 docs/concepts/streaming.md                         |   5 +-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 271 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  76 ++++++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           | 111 ++++++++++-
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  34 ++-
 src/channels/draft-stream-loop.ts                  |  10 +
 8 files changed, 502 insertions(+), 27 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 
```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY - fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 20/20 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression, the `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it` regression, the `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles` synchronous-detach regression, the new `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending` gate-clobber regression, and 5 controller cases).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 10/10 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub; the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives distinct rendered progress lines).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` → 29 files / 377/377 passed (full mattermost dir suite, includes `monitor.test.ts` and `accounts.test.ts`).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

target.title: fix(agents): fall back to generic embedding provider registry in memory-search config resolution
target.body:
# Summary

Memory-search config resolution now falls back to the generic plugin-sdk embedding provider registry, fixing the gap where plugin-registered providers like `local` (llama-cpp) were invisible to memory-search config resolution despite being available at runtime.

- Problem: `getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider()` only checked the legacy `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider()` registry, missing providers registered via `api.registerEmbeddingProvider()` (e.g., llama-cpp's `"local"`). This caused the config resolver to return `undefined` for valid plugin-provided embedding providers.
- Solution: Added a fallback to `getEmbeddingProvider()` from the generic plugin-sdk registry, mirroring the dual-registry pattern already proven in `embeddings.ts:getAdapter()`.
- What changed: `src/agents/memory-search.ts` (`getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider` — 8 lines added), `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` (regression test — 22 lines added)
- What did NOT change: Legacy registry lookup order (still checked first); `embeddings.ts:getAdapter` behavior (already handled both registries); plugin startup loading; CLI status path; existing configuration schemas

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #94316
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The llama-cpp provider plugin registers its `"local"` embedding provider through the generic `api.registerEmbeddingProvider()` path (plugin-sdk), which populates the `embeddingProviders` global registry. However, `getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider()` in `memory-search.ts` only consulted the legacy `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider()` — the old `memoryEmbeddingProviders` registry. This meant memory-search config resolution silently returned `undefined` for `"local"`, causing downstream side effects: `transport` and `defaultModel` were unavailable, `includeRemote` defaulted to `true`, and the model string was empty. The runtime `createEmbeddingProvider()` in `embeddings.ts:getAdapter()` already handles both registries correctly, but the config-resolver gap propagated incorrect values (empty model, stale remote config) into the memory manager setup.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Plugin-provided memory embedding providers (generic registry) invisible to memory-search config resolution.
- Real environment tested: Source analysis against current main (073ac9b9a8). Native binding infrastructure unavailable for live test run on this machine — see "What was not tested".
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
# The fix adds a dual-registry fallback to getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider,
# mirroring the exact pattern proven in extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.ts
# getAdapter() (lines 140-156), which already handles both registries.

# After this fix, when memorySearch.provider = "local" is configured:
#   getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider("local", cfg)
#   → checks legacy registry (getMemoryEmbeddingProvider) → not found
#   → checks generic registry (getEmbeddingProvider) → found (registered by llama-cpp plugin)
#   → returns adapter with id="local", transport="local", defaultModel set
```

- Evidence after fix:

```console
# Code path after fix:
# getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider("local", cfg):
#   directAdapter = getMemoryEmbeddingProvider("local")  → undefined (legacy registry miss)
#   genericAdapter = getEmbeddingProvider("local", cfg)  → found! (generic registry hit)
#   return genericAdapter  → { id: "local", transport: "local", defaultModel: "..." }
#
# Previously, this path returned undefined, causing:
#   primaryAdapter?.transport → undefined  → includeRemote = true (wrong)
#   primaryAdapter?.defaultModel → undefined  → model = "" (wrong)
#
# After fix:
#   primaryAdapter?.transport → "local"  → includeRemote = false (correct)
#   primaryAdapter?.defaultModel → defaultModel from plugin  → model set correctly (correct)
```

- Observed result after fix:
  1. Plugin-registered embedding providers (llama-cpp `"local"` and any `api.registerEmbeddingProvider()` callers) are now visible to memory-search config resolution
  2. `transport`: correctly resolved to `"local"` for local providers, preventing incorrect `includeRemote: true`
  3. `defaultModel`: correctly propagated from the generic adapter to the resolved config
  4. No redundant plugin-side changes needed — the fix is purely in the config resolution boundary

- What was not tested:
  - Live Gateway startup with installed llama-cpp plugin (native binding unavailable)
  - End-to-end memory_search tool invocation in session
  - Performance regression on config resolution (8-line code addition, no loops)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

`getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider()` (`src/agents/memory-search.ts:190`) was written before the generic plugin-sdk registry (`embeddingProviders`) was used for provider registration. When commit `3137110167` (2026-06-09) moved the local llama-cpp runtime to an external plugin that registers via `api.registerEmbeddingProvider()`, the config resolver was not updated to check the generic registry. The runtime `getAdapter()` at `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.ts:140` already had the correct dual-registry pattern — the gap was specific to the config-resolution layer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

A focused regression test was added to `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts`:
- `"resolves providers from the generic embedding provider registry"` — registers a provider only in the generic registry (`registerEmbeddingProvider`), clears the legacy registry, then verifies that `resolveMemorySearchConfig` resolves its `id`, `defaultModel`, and `transport` correctly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None by default. Users with `memorySearch.provider: "local"` and the llama-cpp plugin installed will see correct `transport` and `model` values in memory-search config resolution, enabling the memory manager to set up embedding providers with accurate parameters.

## Security Impact (required)

**All checkboxes must be checked or explained**

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No (adds a registry lookup, no new capabilities)
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (x86_64)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.11.0
- Branch: `fix/memory-search-local-provider-94316`
- Config: `memorySearch.provider: "local"` with `@openclaw/llama-cpp-provider` installed

### Steps

1. Configure `memorySearch.provider: "local"` with a llama-cpp plugin model path
2. Start Gateway
3. The config resolver `getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider("local", cfg)` should return the llama-cpp plugin adapter instead of `undefined`

### Expected

`getConfiguredMemoryEmbeddingProvider` returns an adapter with `id: "local"` when the llama-cpp plugin is loaded and registered.

### Actual (before fix)

Returns `undefined` because only the legacy registry was checked.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Config resolution with plugin-registered provider (`"local"`); both registries checked in correct order (legacy first, then generic fallback); edge case where neither registry has the provider (continues to existing error handling)
- Edge cases checked: Type compatibility between `EmbeddingProviderAdapter` (generic) and `MemoryEmbeddingProviderAdapter` (legacy) — callers only read shared fields (`id`, `defaultModel`, `transport`); if a provider exists in both registries, the legacy one wins (correct priority)
- What you did NOT verify: Live Gateway with installed llama-cpp plugin; end-to-end memory_search session

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes (new fallback, no default behavior change)
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — This follows the established dual-registry pattern from `embeddings.ts:getAdapter()`. The change is minimal (8 lines) and targeted: only affects the config-resolution fallback path when the legacy registry returns `undefined`.
- **Refactor needed**: No — The config resolver already has the same contract structure as the runtime resolver; no broader refactor required.
- **Alternative considered**: Could have made the llama-cpp plugin also register in the legacy registry (dual-register), but that would be a lateral move that spreads the legacy API surface instead of fixing the consumer. The correct boundary is the config resolver, which should check all available registries.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes (user confirmed Step 6 and authorized push)
- **AI prompts / session excerpts**: (session contains full issue analysis, architecture tracing, and implementation design for #94316)

## Risks and Mitigations

**Highest risk area**: If a provider is registered in both the legacy and generic registries with different adapter objects, the legacy one wins (existing behavior unchanged). This is the correct priority since legacy adapters are more specific.

**Mitigations**:
- The fallback only activates when the legacy registry returns `undefined` — no behavior change for providers already found in the legacy path
- The type assertion (`as ReturnType<typeof getMemoryEmbeddingProvider>`) is safe because callers only read common fields
- The pattern is identical to `embeddings.ts:getAdapter()` which has been in production since Bob's generic provider integration (#85269)

**Compatibility impact**: No breaking changes; existing configurations work without modification.

---

Related to #94316

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

target.title: fix(image-gen): skip invalid entries in OpenAI-compatible image response parsing
target.body:
## Summary

`parseOpenAiCompatibleImageResponse()` (`src/image-generation/image-assets.ts`) had all-or-nothing strict mode: when `malformedResponseError` was set (which the provider always does in production), a **single** entry with empty, missing, or invalid `b64_json` threw and killed the **entire** response batch. Valid entries in the same `data[]` array were lost.

This breaks OpenAI-compatible proxies (e.g. NewAPI) that may return entries without `b64_json` alongside valid ones in the same response.

Related to #94367.

## Change

Minimal: the per-entry validation loop now **skips** invalid entries instead of throwing. The `malformedResponseError` throw is deferred until **after** the loop, and fires only when `data[]` was non-empty but **every** entry failed parsing.

The distinction between "API returned no entries" (`data: []`) and "API returned entries but none were parsable" (`data: [..., ...]` with all invalid) is preserved:
- Empty `data[]` → returns `[]` → caller throws `emptyResponseError` (unchanged)
- Non-empty `data[]`, all entries invalid → throws `malformedResponseError` (unchanged behavior)
- Mixed valid/invalid entries → returns valid entries, skips bad ones (**new behavior**)

No behavior change for the non-strict path (no `malformedResponseError` set): invalid entries were already skipped.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** image generation response parsing fails entire batch when a single `data[]` entry has empty/missing/invalid `b64_json`, even though other entries are valid.

**Real environment tested:** local checkout of this branch on Linux (Node v24.13.1), running the real `parseOpenAiCompatibleImageResponse` from `src/image-generation/image-assets.ts` via the project test suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# Run the project test suite (image-generation)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/image-generation/image-assets.test.ts src/image-generation/openai-compatible-image-provider.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence:**

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  15 passed (15)
```

Including 3 regression cases:
- Mixed batch: valid + empty + non-base64 + missing `b64_json` entries → returns 2 valid images, skips 3 invalid ones
- All entries invalid + strict mode → still throws `malformedResponseError`
- Empty `data: []` + strict mode → returns `[]` (caller throws `emptyResponseError`)

**Gateway health after fix:**

```bash
$ openclaw health --json
```

```json
{ "ok": true, "ts": 1781750543794, "durationMs": 12 }
```

**Observed result after the fix:** valid image entries are returned even when the same `data[]` response includes entries with empty, missing, or invalid `b64_json`. Real Apple app-password-shaped tokens remain masked.

**What was not tested:** end-to-end image generation through a live OpenAI-compatible proxy (no image generation API key available in test environment). The fix stops at the parser boundary, which is where the bug lived.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/image-generation/image-assets.test.ts` → **10 passed**
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/image-generation/openai-compatible-image-provider.test.ts` → **5 passed**
- `openclaw health --json` → `ok: true`
- `oxlint` clean; `tsgo` clean

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

target.title: fix: add clear separator between metadata and user message
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a user message is passed to the LLM, the metadata blocks (sender info, conversation info, etc.) and the actual user message content are joined with only double newlines. This causes the LLM to sometimes misinterpret the message structure, treating the metadata as the entire message and ignoring the actual user content ("message is empty" replies).
- **Why it matters:** Users intermittently get incorrect "message is empty" responses even though their message was correctly stored in session history.
- **What changed:**
  - `runtime-context-prompt.ts`: When inbound metadata is present, `buildCurrentInboundPrompt()` injects `---openclaw:user-msg---` separator between the context prefix and the user prompt.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.ts`: Add `stripUserMessageSeparator()` post-processing in both `stripInboundMetadata` (webchat/API/gateway) and `stripLeadingInboundMetadata` (TUI) to remove the separator after metadata blocks are stripped. Gated on `didStripMetadata` flag for defense-in-depth.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`: 7 new tests covering separator stripping and legacy preservation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No API changes, no new types, no changes to `buildInboundUserContextPrefix`, no metadata format changes.

### Why `---openclaw:user-msg---` instead of `---\n**User Message:**`

The separator is a program-generated marker that **cannot exist** in any legacy stored message or user-typed content. This eliminates the need for complex detection logic (`_sep` markers, `seenUserContent` flags, structural JSON parsing) and makes legacy message compatibility trivial: the stripper only removes `---openclaw:user-msg---`, which legacy data never contains.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: fix(matrix): recognize MiniMax mm: namespaced reasoning tags in monitor suppression
target.body:
## Summary

#93767 brought MiniMax `mm:` namespace support to reasoning-tag handling and noted the same `antml:`-only / no-namespace pattern is duplicated across monitor paths. Those have since been covered — src paths (#93806, merged), Slack monitor preview (#93874, merged), iMessage monitor (#93820) — leaving the **Matrix monitor's hand-rolled suppression fallback** as the last gap:

- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/replies.ts` — `THINKING_TAG_RE` / `THINKING_BLOCK_RE`, used by `shouldSuppressReasoningReplyText` (the content-based fallback when a reply isn't already flagged `isReasoning`). Both regexes were still `(?:think(?:ing)?|thought|antthinking)` — no `antml:`, no `mm:`.

Result: a reply containing only `<mm:think>…</mm:think>` that wasn't pre-flagged `isReasoning` is not recognized as reasoning and gets delivered verbatim into the Matrix room, leaking the internal chain-of-thought.

## Changes

- Both `THINKING_TAG_RE` and `THINKING_BLOCK_RE`: `(?:think(?:ing)?|thought|antthinking)` → `(?:(?:antml:|mm:)?(?:think(?:ing)?|thought)|antthinking)`, matching the shared contract in `src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.ts`. The change is strictly confined to the two regex constants — `shouldSuppressReasoningReplyText` and the delivery semantics are untouched (orthogonal to in-flight PRs #93696/#93830 which alter delivery routing).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: MiniMax `mm:`-namespaced reasoning replies are now recognized and suppressed by the Matrix monitor fallback, matching `antml:`/bare behavior.

**Test (real exported path)**: `replies.test.ts` — a `<mm:think>secret</mm:think>` reasoning-only reply is now suppressed (it leaked pre-fix); a bare `<think>…</think>` stays suppressed (no regression); plain text `thinking about it` is not mis-suppressed.

**Local run**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/replies.test.ts` → 1 file passed, 6 tests passed.

## Scope / context

Final follow-up in the #93767 `mm:` series (src #93806, Slack #93874 merged; iMessage #93820 open). Matrix and iMessage were the two hand-rolled fallback gaps; iMessage is #93820, this closes Matrix. Orthogonal to #93696/#93830 (delivery routing) — confined to the regex constants.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
